segments.text: Questions.
Thank you.
Morena koutou, tena koe Madam Chair, members of the
committee, tena koutou.
My name's Stu Kneebone. I'm one of the co-chairs of the Waikato River
Authority. With me is our Kaihautū, CEO,
Anton Coffin.
So look, thank you very much for this opportunity.
We really appreciate you adding these extra schedules of
submitters in. So I'll now hand over to our Kaihautū, Anton, to
take
you through our submission. Thank you.
Good morning, members of the committee.
There's a little bit of trepidation being first up in the morning.
You don't get a feel for what people have said right before you and what's been
happening and get into it. So, a little bit nervous being very first up, but
we'll do our best. Thank you for the opportunity to
present this morning. And with your leave, I'll just get straight into our
presentation.
So
today we're going to
give a summary of our submissions from the Waikato River
Authority. And just as a little bit of background, our authority came
into existence in 2010 as a result of the
Waikato River treaty settlements.
We're a statutory body that was established with
three purposes, one of those being to set the vision
and strategy, Te Tiriti Whai Mana, for the Waikato River,
promote integrated, coordinated, and holistic approach to the management of the
river, and also to fund rehabilitation activities.
So we're part of three settlement acts for the river that cover
the whole river from the Huka Falls all the way up to the mouth
of the Waikato River. There are also five river iwi
post-governance entities that were established as part of the
settlements, and all of them have submitted to the bills
as part of this process.
As I said before, we're an independent statutory body, and we actually got a really
interesting co-governance model where we have five Crown representatives
and five river iwi representatives.
Today, we're advocating for Te Tiriti Whai Mana, the vision and strategy for the
Waikato River. The vision and strategy, Te
Tiriti Whai Mana, was intended by Parliament to be a primary direction-setting
document for the river.
It's a central element of the unique legislation in New Zealand
enacted in relation to the river and its catchment and all it embraces, including
the large catchment of the Waipā River as well.
Our primary interest in the bill is to ensure there's no diminishment of the status
and effect of Te Tiriti Whai Mana or the role of the River Authority in the
management of the Waikato and Waipā River catchments.
Just as a slight aside, we're not technically a post-settlement governance entity.
We were set up as a unique body to be independent, but also
to have roles in collaboration and partnership with the Crown and also
with river iwi.
In terms of some context, as you know, the Waikato catchment is a big
catchment.
It's over a million hectares.
There are longstanding relationships of the iwi and hapū with the lands and
waters. But also, today, farming is a really important part
of the regional economy. Hamilton and Taupō are urban and
industrial growth areas and transport hubs.
And there are many fast-track listed projects and referrals in this area.
So it just sort of talks to people wanting to be in this
area. There's legacy electricity generation,
and long-term municipal takes, both for Waikato and
Auckland. So the river is very, very important to lots of
parts of our area.
Over the last 15 years since our inception, we've made a concerted effort in
restoration, planning, and implementation for the
long-term restoration and health and wellbeing of the Waikato River.
As I've mentioned before, you would have heard from river iwi talking to their
special relationships and connections they have.
As drafted, we feel that the
bills do not uphold the existing treaty settlement arrangements, particularly in
relation to the Waikato and Waipā Rivers.
For the most part, the bills are silent.
In the present system, there are lots of ways that the vision and strategy, Te
Tiriti Whai Mana, connects.
These include when preparing and reviewing regional policy statement, it must be
included.
And it avoids the Schedule 1 process, so it gets
in as a matter of right.
National policy statements and environmental standards, there are direct
links to those.
The Te Tiriti Whai Mana directs consistency of plans prepared by
councils, and a range of different instruments and tools that make
sure that Te Tiriti Whai Mana is at the forefront of all the decisions
that have been made in the planning system.
As well as some of the more recent
advancements in terms of links to wastewater environmental performance
standards, and also land use intensification.
One of the really important roles that we also play is in appointing commissioners.
So we work quite closely with river iwi to make sure that there's a voice
for those people who have a really close connection in some of the
large-scale applications, and that's a process that's been working successfully
for some time.
Turning our thoughts to what changes can be made,
we recommend some changes now rather than later.
And we really think that the committee has an opportunity to set up the bills for
equivalency.
And these are in the areas of the interpretation.
Could be adding the settlement acts in Section 9, having
references to the Waikato River Authority.
We were suggesting a new Section 10A, where you have particular regard
and recognition of Te Tiriti Whai Mana up front.
And in the goals, there could be some recognition of treaty settlements.
We've turned our minds to some of the drafting when we were preparing our
submissions, butThey felt they were very
much narrow in terms of what our role is,
understanding that the treaty settlements are a package,
and they need to be approached in a concerted and comprehensive
way. And so even though we'd put our mind to lots of the drafting,
they needed to also consider what kind of impacts they might
have on other parts of the settlement that are also important.
So what are we requesting? We're asking that the committee change the clauses to
refer to equivalency, and these are in clauses nine and 10 in each
of the bill.
We're thinking that relying entirely on the
post-enactment process to retrofit the treaty settlement arrangement is
an unnecessary risk.
And we recommend the process for secondary legislation waits for the discussions to
be had with our PSGE partners in the catchment.
I certainly, and some of the committee members might remember when the RMA came in,
and it was quite exciting around the hierarchy that was going to be put in, and
we'd have national policy statements, we'd have regional policy
statements, we'd have regional plans, we'd have district plans, and the hierarchy
from the legislation would cascade. But it took two generations to achieve it.
As many will know, the transitional plans took 10, 15
years. Regional policy statements come after, then regional
plans, and then the national policy statements.
So we did it the wrong way around, and it seems like they're learning from that.
This time we could do it the right way around, getting the legislation right,
getting the secondary legislation right, and then it cascades down.
Just to conclude,
we certainly have, you would have seen from the tenor of our submission
that we're really concerned about retrofitting the current treaty settlement
arrangements after the bills are enacted and the secondary legislation and plans
are already in play. And we're asking that the committee recommend that existing
settlements are discussed now in the new system of secondary
legislation.
Certainly, we want to be included in the discussions and the formulation of the
bills as they move through the legislative process, particularly in
regards to the purpose and functions of the River Settlement Acts and the
roles of the Waipato River Authority.
We're thinking there's an opportunity to get things right,
right now, that will set us well up for the next few generations.
And just going to stop there and see if there's any questions you may have.
Look, thank you very much. We have got one question from Lan.
Tena koutou. Thank you so much for your submission.
I wanted to pick up on,
firstly, just your comments around going first this morning.
We were actually in public hearings for a different bill even earlier this morning,
but I wanted to pick up on a number of comments that we've
heard from multiple
iwi and hapū, both settled and non-settled.
And virtually, they talked in different
language about
their concerns about treaty relationships and what
the
passing of these bills might do to that, and one of them used the word
eviscerate treaty relationships.
You're sounding
very pragmatic and constructive in being willing
to work in a way where you think-- It sounds
like you're
keen to get things right now.
Do you think simply more time
in this process would allow you to work with the Crown,
to work with officials, to actually ensure that you're getting it
right, right now? Because as you're likely aware, there's
very
constrained time frames around the government wanting to push this through.
Can you comment on that?
Well, first to acknowledge the sentiment that's
been given to the select committee.
We've come here with a mindset of trying to be really constructive,
and finding a way that we can put our best foot forward to make sure the
treaty settlement's integrity is reflected in the
bills.
And I suppose that
doesn't dismiss the sentiment that we're hearing.
People are being very disappointed
about where are the principles of goodwill and integrity and
good faith. Where are the principles that are
enshrined in the legislation that was passed by Parliament
in terms of the relationship and the partnership with the Crown?
So notwithstanding those things, we're trying to see if there is
a possibility with the time that we do have to get things right.
So yes, time is an important factor to get things right.
You would have seen the benefit of having time in lots of legislation that's passed
quickly. You either spend the time when you're preparing it, or you spend the time
trying to fix it afterwards. And so it seems like we're in that part of the
process. We can make choices. Do we use the time constructively to
get things right, or do we not use the time and then we go onto the other side
and you're in a deficit position where you're trying to
fix things or not be able to fix things, and then the fallout that comes from
those.
Mm-hmm. Look, thank you very much for your time
today. We have met time. We appreciate your submission.
So thank you.
Hey, thanks for the opportunity.
Thank you very much.
Kia ora.
Kia ora.
Kia ora.
So we now move to our next submitter who is here, Choose Clean
Water. Tom, welcome to the select committee
this morning. You've got 10 minutes with us, so we'll hand it over to you.
Cool. Thank you very much. I'm going to focus the things that I speak
to just on a couple of issues. You've heard from a lot of submitters already.
Choose Clean Water has a wide concern and mandate to comment on
water generally, but I'm going to focus specifically on drinking water
protections.
Then I'm going to comment briefly on some of the methods that you're looking at to
protect things like that.And then I've provided you with a PDF document, which may
or may not have made it to you already.
But I can just note some extra things we've put in there that you can look at in
your own time that I don't need to walk you through.
So I'll try and keep things pretty brief.
The first thing I want to talk about or take your minds back to is the Havelock
North drinking water Campylobacter outbreak, which you will know very
well being from Hawke's Bay.
2016, 5,500 cases of Campylobacter illness,
45 hospitalisations, three deaths connected to that outbreak,
$21 million cost to that community,
and ultimately an issue with the drinking water system, with the protection of the
source all the way through to water coming out of people's taps.
Now, this is a massive issue or a growing issue across
the country.
In some ways, unfortunately, what was the biggest case recorded in the world is
potentially at risk of being a small case in the scheme of things.
We've had Selwyn spending $5 million on investigations
to secure their drinking water supply with a potential $400 million
long-term cost. Gore has just built a new pipe under one of their rivers to
dilute their water because it was contaminated with nitrates above drinking water
standards. Sorry, Waimate was. Gore and
Waimate are both building pipes to dilute their water supplies.
And then there's some estimates around places like Christchurch, if their water was
contaminated beyond drinking water standards, that it would cost in the order
of hundreds of millions into the billions of dollars
to deal with their water.
After the Havelock North drinking water Campylobacter outbreak,
there was a report written, an inquiry in 2017, and I've put some
pages from that in a document in front of you.
And I want to draw your attention to a couple of conclusions in that.
Paragraph 617 in particular, the inquiry considers it essential that the
protection of drinking water sources be expressly recognised in the primary
resource management legislation.
So for a government that has
reassured us that it's going to be evidence-based, you have an opportunity here to
take a recommendation from the past and act on that in your resource
management legislation that you're drafting at the moment.
The inquiry goes on to recommend things like amending Section 6, so essentially
making drinking water protection a matter of national importance, to provide
regional councils with explicit direction to protect drinking water sources,
and basically to say, if you don't do this stuff, you need some other form of
national direction that does that. Now, you might say, "Hey, we did that.
We've got a national environmental standard for drinking water.
We've got an NPS for freshwater." The NPS for freshwater does prioritise
human drinking water under Te Mana o te Wai, but the government is
planning or proposing to scrap that hierarchy.
And the bills you have in front of you, the Natural Environment and Planning Bills,
only say that basically, we need to have regard to
drinking water supplies. They do not direct the protection of drinking water
supplies or drinking water sources being rivers, lakes,
aquifers as a matter of national importance.
So I think that's a huge gap in the bill where we risk
significantly degrading our drinking water supplies or essentially having this
continue to be missed 10 years after you were given these recommendations,
and ultimately cost our communities in terms of illness, but
also financially.
Moving on from that to, and you can obviously get advice on that as
you wish, and I recommend you do, but yeah, some strong recommendations there that
I think you should pay attention to.
Moving on from that to some of the tools that you're looking at
using to protect things like that, regardless of whether you decide that you should
only have regard to drinking water sources or whether you should actually protect
them as a matter of national importance.
There have been a lot of comments from submitters on the tools that you're going to
use. Some that have struck me have been around farm plans and
non-regulatory mechanisms. Environment Canterbury, for example, sat
in front of you and said, "Hey, look, farm plans are working quite well in our
region." In their view, a lot of people have A grades.
I want to draw your attention to page five of the documents I've given you,
which is the Ōtūwharekai Ashburton Lakes report written by Ministry for the
Environment. I suggest you get some advice on this too.
But some comments I've highlighted there basically
point out to you that farm plans generally in these places have not been
connected to outcomes. So it's all good for Environment Canterbury to get here and
sit in front of you and say that a lot of their farm plans have A grades.
It doesn't matter if they have an A grade if nothing in that farm plan
is connected to the water quality outcome for the place that we're trying to
protect. So the report specifically says, the farm environment
plan system is overly focused on process rather than environmental outcomes.
So long as farm environment plan A or B grades were achieved,
intensification of high-risk areas near the Ashburton and Ōtūwharekai Lakes were
not monitored or controlled. So these lakes continued to degrade despite the fact
that you had a supposedly robust,
non-regulatory system in place. And if you go to the final page of that
report that I've put in there or page seven in my document, it says the new
National Freshwater Farm Plan system has similarities to the ECAN system, and
that hasn't changed. This report's from a few years ago, but the system that you
are looking at is ultimately going to be similar.
There's other comments in there around lack of information and transparency.
Environment Canterbury has these plans across the region.
They don't have copies of them. They don't know how many cows are operating across
all these catchments. They don't know how much fertilizer is being used across all
these catchments. They have no information readily available to them on the
pressures in these catchments, and that's another issue that the
report pointed out. So I just want you to be aware that you're being told that this
non-regulatory stuff is going to work. The evidence suggests otherwise.
And I'd also point out that farm plans have been something that's been used in New
Zealand since the 1950s for soil conservation and
erosion. We've been told for decades that they will solve our problems, and they
have not. So just be really aware of that.
Finally, I want to draw your attention to a couple of other things that I'll let
you look at in your own time, but I've included two other attachments, C and D.
One, I've heard developers sit in front of you and say, "Look, we don't need much
regulation. It's all red tape. Why would we build under a cliff face?
Why would we build in a floodplain?
We're not that stupid."A couple of cases here where that's happened.
Tangoyo and Hawke's Bay, a developer actually pushed
council for a private plan change to allow them to develop in a floodplain.
It's since been zoned as basically a red zone.
Have a look at that story or get some advice on it.
Another example from Matarangi, where a developer cleared a wetland despite the
NPS saying that that's something that you can't do.
So due diligence just not done despite their assurances.
Finally, we've heard a bit about things like cultural values supposedly getting in
the way of resource consents. It's been something that's come through quite
strongly from Federated Farmers, for example.
I've just included part of the Gore District Plan decision, which basically says
that in that region, in that plan, those concerns are unfounded.
This doesn't get in the way of day-to-day farming activities.
So have a look at that, get some advice on that.
And that's about seven minutes, so maybe if you've got a question or two, I've got
time for them.
Thank you, Tom, for that submission. We'll go to Rachel first.
Great management of your submission time, too.
Yes.
Thank you for that. And you've got a lot in the submission about limits
and the
workarounds and how they
have to be balanced about against other things and the like.
So acknowledging that content, but what I'm interested in asking
you to talk about is what we hear
is if you had a national limit for, say, nitrates, which is
what you refer to, then sometimes...
Well, not even nitrates, but for some things, people will say, well,
the limits are set at a
point that is beyond what a normal environment would
have, even if there was no-
... human interference going.
Yeah.
So
is there a role for targets or some other mechanisms, or where do
you think limits should be set-
Yeah
... in terms of a pristine environment versus-
Yeah. So my view on this is this has been a-
This has been a red herring for over 10 years.
I started getting involved in the national policy statement development back in
sort of 2017-ish, maybe even earlier than that.
Early versions of the national policy statement have always said, if you have a
place that naturally exceeds a limit, that's
fine, it provides for that. It gives you an exception.
The existing 2020 NPS gives you that exception.
So all this rhetoric that we're hearing about streams from pristine environments
exceeding limits, it doesn't matter. It's fine.
The NPS provides for that. I think there's also some misconceptions
about how different New Zealand is as a country.
You're often told, "Hey, we've got to go catchment by catchment and come up with
limits that are really specific." We're not that special. We're not that different.
New Zealand has these diverse landscapes, but from a freshwater
ecology perspective,
from freshwater ecologists, they can pretty confidently say, "Look, this is a
general limit that we should have across our country." There will be exceptions.
There are exceptions provided for in the NPS.
But we shouldn't go back through this, we need it
to be case by case, and then as soon as we try and do that, we get told that that's
unfair, that's different for different regions.
We need it to be a blanket approach.
As soon as we do a blanket approach, we're told that's unfair.
And it's just you're being led down this path for decades, I think,
and it's not that complicated.
Just as supplementary to that, what about
quite leaky soil? So I've got on my office wall the
Greenpeace and Ngāi Tahu map of the South Island,
and the nitrates in Southland and Canterbury are very clearly
different from Otago.
Yeah. And there's a recent report on drinking water risk in Southland that's come
out about those short residence times.
I think there is
some benefit through things like spatial planning.
Southland Plan, for example, looked a lot at the risk profile because of those
soils. And I think that's where spatial planning potentially gives you a huge
advantage to say what types of activities are suitable on
these soils that can then achieve the limits that are
set nationally or whatever it is that you end up doing.
So totally scope for that. The thing that we run into the problem with is where,
and it's happened in Hawke's Bay, for example, we have some very leaky
soils where growing grapes is great, but it requires a huge amount of water.
It's sort of this unfortunate consequence of that landowner
having ended up with that land and growing grapes on that land and then saying,
"Well, I need more water than everyone else because I'm on this old gravel
riverbed." And it's sort of like, well, we actually need to spatially plan to say,
is that a suitable activity to be happening on that land because we're wasting a
lot of water on that space. So yeah.
Okay. Look, Tom, we are going to have to leave it there. We have met time.
We appreciate your time and your submission, so thank you.
Thank you.
Yeah.
So we will now move to our next submitter, Love and
Co.
And I believe Love and Co.- oh, there we go.
Ian and Hamish, welcome to the select
committee this morning.
We will hand it over to you to talk us through your submission, and then we'll open
up for questions.
Thank you, and good morning, members.
The submission by Love and Co., the director, John Love, was unable to join you
today, so he asked Hamish and I to speak to some of the key points.
We are consultants that have done a bit and seen a bit,
and when he asked us for some advice when he was preparing his
submission, we had a few things to say.
So if you're happy, we'll just take you through some of the issues as we see it
and look forward to any questions you have.
And to dive right into it,
and just working through the points that John had made,
I think everyone would agree that adding a concept of being solutions
focused to the bill is a great idea.
But the challenge that we see is if there's
not clarity on what it is councils are being asked to do
as consent authorities,
what solutions might be focused on is hugely ambiguous.
And specifically, if councils
are primarily focused in the
context of a planning consent there to accept an applicant
can make an application for what they want, have it assessed against whether
it meets the relevant plan or legal matters, then being solutions
focused means don't muck around, don't be obtuse, be constructive,
act in good faith, move things forward. And I think everyone would agree with that.
ButBut what Hamish and I sometimes see are council
officers thinking they're environmental gatekeepers,
and we could show you written examples of Section 92
requests where the solutions focus is to ask
applicants to not infringe rules that they're seeking consent for in the first
place as a way to solve that problem.
So we would encourage Parliament to think about, well,
if we want to solve problems, what is the context of the problem being
solved? And if it's about
officers thinking it's their role to take an application and shape it as to
what they think would be a good outcome, well, then being solutions focused
might not help speed things up or help progress things.
If I can take that a step further. John is a developer of
housing, multi-unit housing in Auckland, and
both Ian and I are consultants with 25 to 30 years' experience
each. So we've seen what happens when an act of
Parliament is, in a way, interpreted by council in
such a way that makes it very, very difficult to get things done.
We're working on a job for John right now that's been in council for over a year.
Our solution is that council should not have that ability to make it
take that long for what is 50 or 60 houses
on a site zoned for housing.
Thank you, Hamish. Moving on.
Appreciate that there's a proposal through Section 14 of the bill
to exclude certain effects from consideration.
And the point we'd make there is that if the intent is to exclude
something, it needs to be fully excluded.
And the example in John's submission was the
difference between landscape effects and a landscape character or landscape values
effect, which either needs to be all in or all out, not half in or
half out. And
the point we'd just make about that again is to say it would be
probably negative if positive effects also got excluded from consideration.
Usually trying to find a way to get that betterment is something
that a lot of applicants want to do.
And it may incentivize them to not be so interested in those things if
they can't claim any credit or value from that.
And I think we've got to look at this in context.
What John is talking about here isn't about destroying some
outstanding natural landscape or visual in, say, Central Otago.
We're talking about a highly modified environment of urban Auckland,
where a landscape and visual assessment
really seems unnecessary given the context of what you're building in.
So it's about making sure that we don't
overregulate or provide... We've got to provide a pathway
for getting things done that we want to be done in an area.
Yes. I'm currently dealing with an application for a
high-rise tower in Auckland CBD, subject to an unlimited building height,
and there's a request for a landscape assessment of it, which is difficult for
people to really follow.
One matter we'd also draw your attention to is in proposed Section
38 of the bill, very specific. The way it's written, every
permitted activity must be registered, and to be registered,
one must include a variety of information, including reports from suitable
experts confirming its permitted affected party approvals and so
on. And the likelihood is that a council would then want its own experts to review
an applicant's experts. And there's a real risk of
making permitted activities much harder than they currently are and almost
needing a resource consent to show that you don't need a resource consent.
So we'd absolutely commend to you just being
clear in what circumstances you want permitted activities to
be registered and why.
In principle, it seems to make a bit of sense that everyone can keep track of what
people are doing, but there's a big difference between an applicant just providing
notice to a council that it wishes to undertake a permitted
activity and someone having to seek permission to confirm
that what they want to do is in fact permitted in every single instance.
And an example I'll give you, rather boring one.
I might want to build a fence in a special character area.
The maximum height in a rear or a side fence might be 1.8 meters.
So do I have to go and
show that I'm getting permission to confirm the permitted activity before I build
the fence, or can I build the fence and provide the information later?
Or why would there be any need for me to go and get another piece of paper from
council to confirm the permitted activity of building a fence to the
standard height in that special character area?
It seems to us to be adding paperwork to the process,
not taking it away.
We have a lot of experience
through resource consents or planning approvals, as they may become,
and particularly with requests for further information,
requests for peer reviews, use of things like specialist urban design
panels or design review panels, et cetera.
And I think the point that John's making is not that these
things are inappropriate or wrong, but really need to be subject to
clearer frameworks and guidelines.
The Auckland Urban Design Panel, for instance, is now mandatory,
including four to six weeks wait in some cases, and there's just no
negotiation about that. Even where an applicant has obtained specialist reports
and the council has specialist reports, it still wants that, and
there's simply no way out of that framework.
So the suggestion was that those things, as good as they can be, should
remain fully voluntary, and for applicants to also wish to be
part of, rather than, for instance, spending money on their own consultants if they
wish. Related to that, we see a lot, again, the request for further
information. We really would support a requirement that any person
invited to give a comment or review an application
really should be suitably qualified and experienced.
This is a lot of time and money getting second opinions or third opinions or fourth
opinions, and really, it should be about who's the
most suitably qualified to give that assurance, rather
than do you happen to be part of team council or team
applicant?
Further on that is the independence of some of these boards.
They'll often get confidential council officer briefings beforehand,
and it sometimes feels as if you're being tried before
a jury where you've got no chance of winning.
So we would express that they need to be independent
and separate from council in a way so that we
can get good outcomes.
Just because a council officer doesn't like the proposal doesn't mean it's got
merit, and doesn't mean it shouldn't be approved.
And just on that point of independence,
we're aware that independent commissioners get widely used.
We're aware, especially in the last six months or so, there's been some very
well-documented public discussions around the nature and
competence of some of those commissioners and how they become commissioners and who
appoints them, et cetera.
We would say there's huge support for independent commissioners and
the assistance they can provide everyone, whether they're a submitter or a
council or an applicant, or just a member of the community wanting confidence in
the system. But
we would say that where there is a real opacity in the current
system is the appointment process of how specific commissioners
find themselves appointed in specific instances or not.
And we would absolutely encourage a strengthening of independent
commissioner use across the country, including
possibly improving the training they receive, possibly
helping councils make appointments in a consistent and fully
independent way.
And
in the case of things that aren't public hearings, like making
notification decisions, it's really uncertain and unpredictable when
a council will let an applicant have an independent commissioner make that
decision, and when a council says, "No.
We don't have to, so the answer's no."
And it's something we again think could be made much more transparent for
everyone's benefit.
So on that-
Thank you, Ian. I'm just going to jump in there because you have almost met time.
And there is a question here, but if you want to potentially wrap it up,
if you can, that would be great.
Oh, look, the last thing we'll say then is just the planning tribunal idea.
Really support it. I think there's always been a gap between going
to a High Court or Environment Court, which is a huge process,
and the limitations of the current Section 357 objection process.

And I understand the problems with crack willows, but you're talking
about the purple willows as well. On this photo, are
these baby willows or are the willows further out?
I didn't mention this, but this is probably a critical point.
I was in a meeting with regional council and a whole lot of ecologists and others,
and my gosh, amongst us, we think this willow
weed is the biggest threat we've ever seen in terms of weeds of pest in New
Zealand. It's taking over whole braid plains of riverbeds.
I had regional council staff, senior staff out there a couple of weeks ago.
Couldn't believe it. Whole braid plains.
So the crack river grows on the sides of the willows, of the rivers.
It doesn't grow through the middles of the braid plains that much.
Whereas this willow, it's a purple willow, it's a shrub type willow.
It's taken over whole braid plains of riverbeds.
It's absolutely frightening.
It's this low stuff on the... It's the-
Yeah. It's all the low stuff in the middle of the photo.
So on the left, far left and the far right, you can probably just see some crack
willows.
Yeah.
But all through the middle of that photo, that used to be clear braid plain where
river nesting birds used to nest. So that's why we
say with the whole legislation, the legislation's almost,
we're so far behind what we're dealing with at the coal face here.
We're dealing with massive environmental disasters here.
Deer and pigs like on this farm here.
It's just out of control, and we've got
legislation that's not getting us in front of
all this environmental damage that we're seeing.
Question. Okay. Thank you very much, Jamie, Bryce, and Laurie.
We appreciate your time today.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
So that brings us, members, to the end of our public
session,
and so we will now move into private session.
